summary: Jeff Atwood announces the birth of his son Henry Burton Atwood, framing the experience through programming metaphors. He credits his partner Betsy for doing the 'real work' and provides a vivid, humorous description of the pain scale during childbirth. The post celebrates the arrival with geeky touches, including a C++ book photo op and creating a Twitter account for Henry with the message 'Hello World.' Atwood concludes by noting he'll approach parenting the same way he approaches programming—with no idea what he's doing.
key_points: The birth announcement is framed as 'spawning a new process' with the baby assigned a process id
Atwood provides a darkly humorous description of childbirth pain that goes well beyond a 10/10 scale
The post references the Mac vs PC ad featuring a C++ GUI Programming book as a joke gift
Henry's first tweet was 'Hello World'—the classic first program output
Atwood admits he approaches both parenting and programming with no idea what he's doing
money_quotes: All credit for creation of this new process of course goes to my partner Betsy who did the real work.
Now imagine taking that pain, packing it in to a rickety Oldsmobile, setting it on fire, then driving it off the edge of a cliff at maximum speed. Into a valley filled with white-hot molten lava.
It's a little bit worse than that.
It's all ponies and butterflies. Until the ponies and butterflies start spontaneously screaming.
Henry, who is genetically engineered to be awesome.
I will approach our new parenting adventure the same way I do programming – with absolutely no freaking idea what I'm doing.
key_insight: Life's most profound experiences—like becoming a parent—can be approached with the same humility and humor as learning to code: acknowledging you have no idea what you're doing, but diving in anyway.
